title: Comment from Melewski, Ryan, HHS-ONC-2025-0005, HHS-ONC-2025-0005-0001, 2025-23896
comment_text:
Comment of Ryan Melewski, Esq.
Submitted to: ASTP/ONC — Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions to Unleash Prosperity (HTI-5) Proposed Rule
Federal Register Docket: 2025-23896
Comment Period: December 29, 2025 – February 27, 2026

Dear ASTP/ONC Representatives,

I submit this comment as an Ohio attorney deeply committed to patient safety, accountability, and the integrity of electronic health information. I am writing as an individual, not on behalf of any organization, to express serious concerns about components of the Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions to Unleash Prosperity (HTI-5) Proposed Rule.

At its core, the HTI-5 proposal aims to streamline and reduce burdens within the ONC Health IT Certification Program by eliminating or revising a significant number of certification criteria, and by resetting the regulatory scope for future interoperability standards. Among these changes are proposed removals of long-standing auditing and privacy/security functionality that has served as a safeguard for patients and clinicians.

Let me be clear: innovation cannot come at the expense of truth in the clinical record. An audit trail — the digital footprint of who accessed, created, edited, or deleted health data — is not an optional feature. It is the foundation of trust in electronic health information. Removing or weakening requirements for auditable events, audit reports, and auditable actions on health information would reduce transparency and remove essential tools for identifying errors, preventable harm, and potential fraud.

The proposed rule contemplates removing certification criteria in the Privacy and Security domain, including auditable events and tamper resistance (§ 170.315(d)(2)), audit report(s) (§ 170.315(d)(2)), and auditing actions on health information (§ 170.315(d)(10)). These capabilities are not antiquated burdens — they are critical to ensuring the security, accountability, and integrity of patient data in an era of increasing digital threats and data complexity.

Without reliable audit data, it becomes exponentially more difficult — if not impossible — to reconstruct what happened when a patient’s clinical information is altered. This is not hypothetical. Accurate audit trails are essential for clinicians to verify data used in diagnosis and treatment, for patients to understand their own care histories, and for regulatory and legal investigations that identify systemic risks and prevent future harm. Dismantling these safeguards shifts risk onto patients and erodes public confidence in health IT.

Furthermore, the HTI-5 rule should not proceed to remove fundamental certification protections before updated standards for data creation, preservation, and exchange are firmly in place. Interoperability that emphasizes data movement without protecting how that data is governed, audited, and preserved invites clinical and administrative chaos — exactly the opposite of what patients deserve.

My recommendations are straightforward:

Retain and reinforce audit trail certification criteria — including auditable events, audit reports, and audit trail functionality — until modernized standards are codified that preserve meaningful accountability.

Ensure definitions and standards for key EHI concepts (such as audit logs, tamper resistance, and traceability) are updated so that patients, clinicians, auditors, and regulators have consistent, enforceable expectations for how data is created, accessed, and shared.

Do not remove privacy and security safeguards until there are robust mechanisms to ensure that data integrity and patient access remain protected in all interoperable environments.

Recognize that deregulation must not equate to defanging the tools that reveal systemic risk, prevent harm, and protect taxpayers.

The promise of interoperability, and of AI-enabled innovation, is real. But innovation without accountability is not progress — it is peril. Public trust in healthcare data depends on transparency that cannot be achieved without meaningful audit requirements and clear standards that hold health IT to account.

I urge ASTP/ONC to reconsider any deregulatory action that would undermine the integrity, security, and accountability of electronic health information. The stakes are too high — patient safety, clinical validity, and the basic right of every person to trust their health information depend on it.

Respectfully submitted,
Ryan Melewski, Esq.
